slides.subtitleText: New here? Don't worry — this section walks you through everything step by step. No experience needed.
slides.bodyText: When learning security, you don't want to practice on your real computer. Instead, you set up a virtual machine (VM) — think of it as a computer inside your computer. You can break it, reset it, or delete it without touching your actual system.

Security tools mostly run on Linux. You don't need to install Linux on your real machine — you'll run it inside a VM (explained in Step 2).
If you're a complete beginner → start with Kali Linux. It comes with everything preinstalled.
bullets: OS  ::  Best for  ::  Download
Kali Linux  ::  Beginners — 600+ security tools preinstalled, tons of guides online  ::  ISO / VM image
Parrot OS  ::  Beginners who want something lighter than Kali  ::  ISO / VM image
BlackArch  ::  Experienced users — 3000+ tools, Arch-based  ::  ISO / VM image
Garuda Linux  ::  Arch users who want a nice desktop and can add BlackArch tools  ::  ISO
Arch Linux  ::  Advanced users who want to build everything from scratch  ::  ISO / VM image

slides.titleText: Step 2 — Set Up a Virtual Machine
slides.subtitleText: Not sure which to pick? Use VirtualBox — it's free, runs on Windows/macOS/Linux, and has thousands of tutorials.
slides.bodyText:
A virtual machine lets you run a second operating system (like Kali Linux) as a window on your current computer. Your real system is never touched.
Pick the VM software for your computer:
#### Installing VirtualBox
On Windows: Download the installer from virtualbox.org and run it like any other program.
On macOS:
(If you don't have Homebrew yet: brew.sh)
On Ubuntu / Debian Linux:
bullets: Software  ::  Your computer runs...  ::  Cost  ::  Best for
VirtualBox  ::  Windows, macOS, Linux  ::  Free  ::  Beginners — easiest to get started
VMware Workstation Pro  ::  Windows, Linux  ::  Free (personal)  ::  Slightly faster than VirtualBox
VMware Fusion  ::  macOS  ::  Free (personal)  ::  Mac users on Intel chips
Parallels Desktop  ::  macOS  ::  Paid  ::  Mac users on Apple Silicon (M1/M2/M3)
GNOME Boxes  ::  Linux  ::  Free  ::  Linux users who want the simplest option
Virt-Manager / KVM  ::  Linux  ::  Free  ::  Linux users who want maximum performance

slides.titleText: Step 2 — Set Up a Virtual Machine
slides.codeText: sudo apt update && sudo apt install -y virtualbox

slides.titleText: Step 2 — Set Up a Virtual Machine
slides.bodyText:
#### Creating your first VM (Kali Linux example)
That's it — Kali boots up in a window. Default login: kali / kali.
#### Recommended settings (adjust after importing)
Right-click your VM → Settings:
#### Snapshots — your "undo" button
Before you install tools or start experimenting, take a snapshot. If anything goes wrong, you can roll back instantly.
In VirtualBox: Machine → Take Snapshot → name it clean-base.
To restore: Machine → Restore Snapshot → clean-base.
bullets: Download the Kali Linux VirtualBox image — pick the VirtualBox version (.ova file).
Open VirtualBox → File → Import Appliance → select the .ova file → click Import.
Select the new VM and click Start.
Setting  ::  What to set  ::  Why
System → Memory  ::  4096 MB (4 GB) or more  ::  Less than 4 GB and tools will be slow
System → Processors  ::  2 CPUs  ::  Keeps things responsive
Storage → Disk  ::  60 GB+ (dynamically allocated)  ::  Tools take up space quickly
Network → Adapter 1  ::  NAT  ::  Gives the VM internet access
General → Advanced → Clipboard  ::  Bidirectional  ::  Lets you copy/paste between VM and host

slides.bodyText:
If you want to install Linux directly on a physical computer instead of a VM:
This updates all software before you start.
bullets: Download the ISO for your chosen OS.
Flash it to a USB drive using Balena Etcher — just pick the ISO and your USB stick, click Flash.
Reboot the computer, boot from USB (usually press F12 or F2 at startup), and follow the on-screen installer.
After installation, open a terminal and run:
slides.codeText: sudo apt update && sudo apt full-upgrade -y && sudo reboot
